sentences.text: Yes.
Good morning.
Gerda Marcus Wilchfort.
My maiden name is Blachmann, Gerda Blachmann.
Gerda is G-E-R-D-A, Marcus, M-A-R-C-U-S, Wilchfort, W-I-L-C-H-F-O-R-T, and Blachmann is B-L-A-C-H-M-A-N-N.
Yes.
24th of April, 1923.
I was born in Breslau in Silesia in Germany.
It was Germany then.
Today, it's Wroclaw in Poland.
It's-- they-- but when I was born, it was Breslau in German.
B-R-E-S-L-A-U.
Yes.
Breslau was a very nice, middle-size town in Germany.
It had a very famous rabbinical seminary.
And our Rabbi Brieger here, from the synagogue, was educated there.
And it was a old town.
It had an old dome.
It had a beautiful inner city, very old inner city.
It had a promenade with a little river going around.
It was a very lovely city.
And it had a good-sized Jewish community and a beautiful synagogue before Kristallnacht, with a dome, with two rabbis, and Jewish Hebrew school, of course, and Jewish gymnasium.
And people lived very comfortably, more modest than in the United States, but comfortable and-- and harmonious.
And before Hitler came, we didn't feel too much anti-Semitism.
Well, the percentage of Jews in Germany was altogether very small.
So I'm not real sure how-- what the percentage was, but I'm sure not more than 5% of the population.
Yeah.
I grew up in-- in apartments.
Most people lived in apartments.
Very few people had homes like here.
And it was [?
Gutenbergstrasse ?] 28, I remember, I grew up in.
It was on the outskirts and near my school.
The-- the-- first four years of school, the elementary school was in walking distance, very close by.
And I remember that, yeah.
Yes, certainly.
I had three grand-- I had-- my mother's parents lived near the synagogue.
And-- because they were quite religious, and I don't think my grandparents would take the trolley car to-- they-- they walked to synagogue.
And we spent all the holidays there.
My grandmother was a very good cook, and she made Seders and Rosh Hashanah dinners.
And I remember that very well.
The family always assembled there.
I had one uncle who was married with one son.
He came.
And-- but otherwise, both my parents-- my parents-- no, I'm sorry.
My father was an only child, and my mother had only that one brother.
And I was an only child.
I didn't have any brothers and sisters.
Tebel, Jenny and Louis Tebel.
And my grandmother's was-- my father's mother was Elfaide Blachmann.
And she was a widow, and later on she lived with us for quite a while.
We had later a bigger apartment and close to the Jewish gymnasium where we later went.
And she lived with us for a few years, which was not always easy, but she did live with-- oh.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Just take the receiver.
Can we cut?
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Right.
Well, my grandmother on my mother's-- on my mother's side was a little woman.
And she had-- she always wore glasses.
So the glasses always fell over.
And then [?
Seder ?] at night, she-- when the children were unruly or talked, so she always looked over the glasses.
And she was a very good cook and good mother.
But in-- in this context, I don't have any [? thought. ?]
Arthur Blachmann.
He was a salesman.
He had a distributorship of iron [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] articles from-- from Stanley Work.
Stanley Work is today in America very big, and it had already a factory then in America and in Germany.
And he had distributorship for Silesia for Stanley and other firms.
He was a very mellow, gentle man.
He was in World War I as a soldier, of course, and as a medical-- medical personnel.
But he would never, never talk about it, and he would never answer questions about it.
He was-- he just as soon forgot about it, and he-- he wouldn't talk.
He talked very little altogether.
And the upbringing of children was completely different.
The parents were-- the fathers, mostly, were quite remote.
And I just got closer to him as I grew up.
When I was little, I understand he was very proud of me.
But when he-- in the middle years, he didn't talk very much.
And he didn't have this give and take that American fathers have with their children, unfortunately.
My mother's name was Erna, Erna Blachmann, maiden name Tebel.
And she was very loving and very close to me.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we did-- had a lot of fun.
She was very young when I was born, and we had a lot of fun when I grew up.
We stood sometimes in front of the mirror and made faces.
And she was very close to me.
No, not really, no.
No.
Not very much, to be honest.
[LAUGHS] My grandmother lit candles, and maybe we went there for Friday night dinner.
But I don't remember it that much.
I just mostly remember the holidays.
Yes.
Yeah.
Because we didn't have a kosher home, and my grandmother wouldn't eat in our home.
No.
Well, my mother always said I was too serious and too good.
[LAUGHS] Because I was a single child, and I was pretty good in school.
And when we-- she always complained that I was too goody-goody.
She wanted me to be more lively.
But it was-- you know, when-- I was only 10 years old when Hitler came to power, and I-- I was very much affected by that.
And we were a very serious generation of young people.
We couldn't-- we weren't all that silly and all that, you know, like that.
We were serious from the beginning, I think.
Well, like I said, I went to this elementary school.
And after four years in Germany, you make a test and go to a lycee, so a lyceum.
So I went to a Catholic school for girls to start the gymnasium part.
But after two or three years of Hitler, they wouldn't let Jewish kids go to regular schools anymore.
So I went to the Hebrew gymnasium, which was very good.
It was accredited as a high school, like-- but in Germany it was called gymnasium.
But I was very ambitious.
I-- I knew Hebrew, but not enough for this school.
So I went by trolley car a few times a week to a tutor to learn more Hebrew so I could get into the right classes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very nice.
We had already boys and girls together then in this school.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was good.
Well, in the beginning it was fine, you know.
But then when Hitler came-- some girls were very nice about it.
But most girls right away started to discriminate, and they didn't want to sit next to me.
And they'd say, Jews-- Jewish children smell, and they wouldn't.
It started right away.
They had-- I had very few friends.
There were a few other Jewish children in that school.
But the discrimination started right away.
Well, I always figured I'd go to college and become some kind of professional.
But it became clear very quickly that I will not be able to do that.

So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after I couldn't-- when it became clear that we had to leave Germany eventually, my parents sent me to a school for girls to learn household, and near-- that was called Wolfratshausen.
That was near Munich.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there we learned how to cook and to clean.
And we had some academic courses, but mostly it was for household duties.
And that was very orthodox, you know.
We-- we-- it was kosher, and we couldn't have meat.
They-- there was no more kosher meat in Germany by that time.
So we had mostly potatoes and noodles and rice, and I came home 20 pounds heavier than I went.
But I-- I learned that I was very unhappy there because I didn't quite fit in with the other girls.
And I was there about eight months, from April.
In the summer, we have summer vacation in-- in Germany.
So I was home for six weeks, and I went back.
And in November, the-- the Kristallnacht started.
No.
No, not many.
I had mostly-- well, I-- I was small, you know.
I was a little girl.
So I had mostly Jewish friends, the children of my parents' friends.
I don't remember non-Jewish friends in particular.
And in the elementary school, I wasn't hated, or I didn't have any disputes there.
But friends, I had mostly Jewish friends.
Well, like I said, in school we had the discrimination.
And I had to leave-- pretty soon I had to leave the Catholic lyceum, which was not bad.
The teachers, and especially the principal, were very nice, and they were not Nazis.
They wanted to keep the Jewish children.
I remember that the-- the principal called me into the office and told me, or told my parents, that she's very sorry, but she cannot keep us.
And she was smart enough.
She said, don't worry.
We are the next.
Because Hitler hated the-- the Catholics, too.
And she said, but right now I cannot do anything.
I cannot keep the Jews.
I'm not allowed to keep the Jewish children.
So that's when I went to the Jewish gymnasium.
Well, I don't know.
That wasn't too bad.
Because the Jewish school was very good, was also close by, and it was very good.
We learned a lot.
We probably learned more than in the Catholic [INAUDIBLE].
And you felt more secure, you know, in your own environment.
You didn't have the attacks.
And you didn't have to worry what the other children will say to you or do to you.
I think I felt pretty secure there.
Oh, yes, yes.
That's-- like I said, I was in-- in that school far away from home in-- near Munich in this-- and was in a [? little ?] village, Wolfratshausen.
It was a house, a big house, like a big hotel.
And we were about 100 girls, only girls.
And we had heard already on the radio about the-- assassination of this-- of this-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] ambassador.
It wasn't really the ambassador.
It was-- he-- he got the wrong man.
But still, at Paris, was an assassination.
And we heard on the radio already that how they started to provoke something against the Jews.
And we had our doubts.
That's what I mean.
I was only 15 by then, and we-- we knew, and we were quite aware of what's going on.
So on the 10th of November, at 6 o'clock in the morning the SS stomped into our-- into this school, into this house.
We were all asleep.
And they went in all the dormitories and all the bedrooms and said, raus, raus, raus, up!
And we had to get dressed and leave all our belongings, whatever we had with us-- clothing, bicycles, books.
And we had to march outside, stand outside the house.
And the direction had the, you know, the director of this home had to make sure that we were sent home.
They closed the home-- this school.
And it took a while because there were children from all over Germany there-- there.
And I was really from the furthest away.
From Munich to Breslau, at that time almost eight hours traveling time on the train.
And we didn't have any money.
And they didn't have enough money on hand to buy 100 train tickets all of a-- so suddenly.
So it took a while until this all got organized, and I think we went to Munich.
And by noon or so, I got on a train all by myself.
I think I was the only girl from Breslau.
And we knew something terrible has happened, but we really didn't know what.
So I traveled on that train.
And I had to change in a little town called [?
Hove, ?] and I off.
I had some money on-- you know, from my parents.
So I went, and my-- my connection wasn't till 2:00 in the morning or 5:00 in the morning.
So I was smart enough.
I got into a little hotel because I didn't want to stay on the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] station.
And they woke me up, and I went by myself then at 4:00 or 5:00 in the-- to the train and got on the train.
All of a sudden, there come two big SS men on the train and started to brag what wonderful things had just happened.
Did you see how the synagogues are burning?
Did you see all that glass all over?
How the people smash the store windows?

And we got so many people, and we beat them up, and they were-- and I was sitting there, 15 years old, all alone and shivering, and not knowing what's going to happen to my parents and how I'm going to come.
So when I came to Breslau about 2:00 in the early afternoon, the-- the station, I took a trolley car to station.
And the-- the street passes-- almost passes by the synagogue, and the synagogue was still burning.
I saw the synagogue burning, and I saw all that glass on the-- on the street.
They had quite a number of Jewish stores and businesses on the main streets.
And I was scared.
Well, I came home, and nobody was home.
So-- but finally my mother came home, and she told me that my father had been hiding at my widowed grandmother, because the SS went to all Jewish homes and grabbed the men.
But they didn't go to the-- you know, my grandmother lived alone at that time.
So they were-- my grandfather and my father were hiding there at this [? thing. ?] And they were spared.
They were not taken.
But hundreds of Jewish men were taken to a concentration camp for the first-- that's the first time we heard about concentration camps.
And I think Sachsenhausen or one was-- was one of the first ones.
We had cousins taken and friends taken.
And that was quite a bad time.
So after three, four days, they came home again, and we started to look for a country to go.
Then finally it dawned on them that they have to go.
Because my father worked for this Stanley Works.
But-- and the director of the Stanley Works in-- was in [?
Velbert ?] in the Rhineland.
He knew that my father was Jewish.
My mother had told them.
They went sometimes on trips together, and so my mother had told his wife.
And he was very, very nice and anti-Nazi.
And he said, Blachmann, don't worry about it.
We'll-- you know, you will not be affected.
You will not to be affected.
If-- if it comes to the worse, we'll put Christian and Aryan, like they called it Aryan, men into the office, into the business.
And you just pay them a salary, and it will still be yours.
And we'll support you, and don't worry about it.
But I had my doubts.
And I was crying at night, and I was imploring them, forget about the business and let's go.
Let's go somewhere.
Let's get out.
Because I always had the feeling that somehow they want to kill all the Jews.
But I didn't think of gas, and I didn't think of this.
I always had the feeling they're not going to give us any food.
Because there was already-- started already to be a little bit of food shortages after Hitler, when after Hitler came.
Wasn't bad, but we had already shortages.
And I always said if it gets worse, they're not going to give food stamps or food to the Jews, and they're going to starve us to death.
And as long as my father made a living, he was not to be persuaded.
He said, you're a young kid.
You don't know.
And they wouldn't believe me.
I was very scared.
I knew that this was going to end very badly.
I had-- I felt it in my gut, so to speak.
And when I came back from the Wolfratshausen, I didn't really have much to do.
I had-- couldn't go back to school, and it was not very nice.

But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most of-- you know, most of all I remember that my parents then finally understood that they have to get out of Germany and that they have to find a country, which was very difficult.
I think Kristallnacht, yeah, and the camp.
Cousins came back from this concentration camp.
I remember one cousin lived in the province in-- in [?
Brieg. ?] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he came back from the concentration camp with the shaved hair and skinny and terribly upset.
And so-- so we knew.
